name: Gerstner-Programm
classification_notes: Rooted in Akzidenz-Grotesk, Gerstner-Programm is a systematically expanded grotesque family developed for phototypesetting; its parametric grid of widths and weights places it firmly in the Swiss grotesque tradition.
summary: A digitisation of Karl Gerstner's 1964–67 phototypesetting grotesque, itself a rigorous parametric expansion of Akzidenz-Grotesk developed for H. Berthold AG.
description: Originally conceived as a systematic revision of Akzidenz-Grotesk, Gerstner-Programm applied strict mathematical constants to coordinate widths, weights and italics into a coherent family grid. Gerstner defined four gradations each for width and weight, scaled by a factor of 1.25, with italics set at 12 degrees to the vertical. Stephan Müller's digitisation, completed between 2007 and 2017, preserves the structural logic of the original eight styles across Light, Regular, Medium and Bold with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: Forgotten Shapes situate Gerstner-Programm within the context of 1950s and '60s Swiss typography, where clear shapes, grid-based design and so-called neutral letterforms were dominant. They describe how Karl Gerstner analysed seven common sans serifs before selecting Akzidenz-Grotesk as his foundation, transforming its historical heterogeneity into a homogeneous, parametrically structured system produced in collaboration with H. Berthold AG for the Diatype phototypesetting system.

name: Reform-Grotesk
classification_notes: A revival of D. Stempel A.G.'s early 20th-century Reform-Grotesk, a German grotesque of the mechanised type production era; the subcategory follows directly from its historical lineage as a classic German grotesque sans.
summary: A scholarly revival of Schriftgießerei D. Stempel's early 20th-century grotesque, researched and digitised by Pierre Pané-Farré across a decade of archival work.
description: Based on D. Stempel A.G.'s Reform-Grotesk, first produced around 1904 through the foundry's pioneering mechanical punch-cutting processes, this revival also incorporates the typeface's later incarnation as Information. The family spans Thin through Bold with matching italics and a Semi Extended cut, giving it range across both text and display settings. Forgotten Shapes situates the release within a detailed account of early 20th-century German type production, tracing the design through anonymous collaboration, acquisition and formal revision over several decades.
foundry_blurb: Forgotten Shapes present Reform-Grotesk as a historically complex typeface whose origins at Schriftgießerei D. Stempel are more layered than the commonly cited 1904 date suggests. Developed during the shift to mechanised type production, it later reappeared under the name Information before disappearing with the advent of phototypesetting and digitisation. Pierre Pané-Farré's revival, developed between 2015 and 2024, draws on archival research into surviving patterns, specimens and machine documentation from the period.

name: Affichen-Schriften
classification_notes: A revival family drawn from nineteenth-century poster typefaces including antiques, egyptiennes and fat faces; the category is display given every member was designed explicitly for poster and bill work at large sizes.
summary: A family of revivals drawn from nineteenth-century German and continental European poster typefaces, researched and released by Pierre Pané-Farré between 2011 and 2017.
description: Affichen-Schriften gathers several distinct revival typefaces sourced from historical specimens by foundries including W. Thorowgood, E. Haenel and E. Tarbé & Cie, spanning the period 1821 to c. 1850. The constituent members include antiques, egyptiennes and wide fat-face cuts, each reflecting the formal vocabulary that emerged on continental European bills and posters as English poster types crossed the Channel. The accompanying research essay by Pané-Farré traces the largely undocumented dissemination of poster types through German-speaking printing industries up to around 1860.
foundry_blurb: Forgotten Shapes frames Affichen-Schriften as a revival project rooted in the formal genesis of the nineteenth-century poster, drawing on surviving type specimens, extant sorts and trade journals to reconstruct typefaces used in German and continental European poster printing. The family spans four named sub-families and is accompanied by an extensive historical essay tracing the introduction of English poster aesthetics into the German-speaking world.

name: Lector
classification_notes: The name and foundry context suggest a reading-oriented serif; classified as a text serif based on the family name 'Lector' (Latin for reader) and Forgotten Shapes' focus on refined, functional typefaces.
summary: A text serif from Forgotten Shapes built around legibility and reading comfort, with its name drawn from the Latin word for reader.
description: Lector is a text-oriented serif from Berlin-based Forgotten Shapes, its name signalling a clear focus on sustained reading. The family includes OpenType features and broad language support within the Latin script, suggesting careful attention to typographic utility. It fits naturally in book, editorial and long-form setting where a dependable, characterful text face is needed.
foundry_blurb: Forgotten Shapes present Lector as a text serif with a full character set, OpenType features and language support, available to try via a trial font before purchase.

name: Monument
classification_notes: A revival of an 1903 Berlin foundry sans serif with even stroke width, high-waisted narrow capitals and abrupt angular shoulders; fits squarely within the historical grotesque tradition of German jobbing typefaces.
summary: A scholarly revival of Schriftgießerei Emil Gursch's 1903 Berlin grotesque, reconstructed by Stephan Müller and Reymund Schröder with five weights and matching italics.
description: Originally cut in 1903 for Berlin's booming commercial print trade, Monument is a sans serif of even stroke width and high-waisted, relatively narrow capitals, with a defining characteristic of abrupt angular shoulders on arched letters such as 'h', 'n' and 'u'. Forgotten Shapes rebuilt the family across five weights from Light to Heavy, each with a corresponding italic, staying faithful to the original's distinctive geometry while making it viable for contemporary use. The revival is accompanied by detailed historical research into the Berlin type foundry scene of the early twentieth century.
foundry_blurb: Forgotten Shapes present Monument as a revival of a sans serif first released by Schriftgießerei Emil Gursch in Berlin in 1903, one of two stylistically paired designs produced alongside Consul from the Woellmer foundry that same year. The typeface is described as having strict, classical forms with even stroke width, high-waisted narrow capitals, square dots, small apertures and characteristically abrupt angular shoulders. The digital revival was carried out by Stephan Müller and Reymund Schröder between 2015 and 2021, and comprises five weights from Light to Heavy with matching italics.

name: Kleukens-Egyptienne
classification_notes: A slab serif revival derived from the Clarendon genre, characterised by sturdy, low-contrast strokes and squared slab serifs without round bracketing, placing it firmly in the egyptienne tradition.
summary: A revival of an unpublished 1929 slab serif by Friedrich Wilhelm Kleukens, reconstructed by Reymund Schröder from a low-resolution trial proof and three surviving sketch sheets.
description: Originally designed for D. Stempel AG but never released, the Kleukens-Egyptienne sat unknown in archives until the trial proof was discovered and later considered lost. Schröder's 2018 revival draws on scarce surviving materials to reconstruct a design that departs from conventional Clarendon models by eliminating round bracketing and drop terminals in favour of crisper, more rectilinear forms. The result is five weights with matching italics, occupying an unusual position between book typography and geometric sobriety within the slab serif tradition.
foundry_blurb: Forgotten Shapes describe Kleukens-Egyptienne as an unpublished typeface by Friedrich Wilhelm Kleukens, dating to 1929 and held in the D. Stempel AG archives. The original proof is now considered lost; the revival by Reymund Schröder in 2018 was made possible by a low-resolution scan and three signed sketch sheets unearthed at the Klingspor Museum. The design takes the Clarendon genre as its starting point but strips out soft bracketing and drop terminals in favour of more matter-of-fact, precise forms, making it an outlier in Kleukens' otherwise contrast-oriented body of work.

name: Texpo
classification_notes: The name references the Expo 64 context and the page lists it alongside Kleukens-Egyptienne, suggesting a slab or egyptienne classification typical of mid-century Swiss display and text typography.
summary: A revival of a typeface originally designed by Gret Mengelt-Mergenthaler for the Swiss National Exposition of 1964, revised in 2018 with Christian Mengelt.
description: Originally created for Expo 64 in Lausanne, this revival recovers a typeface tied to one of Switzerland's most significant post-war public events. The 2018 revision was undertaken together with Christian Mengelt, preserving the original design's mid-century Swiss character. Currently available in a single Regular weight, it sits within the broader Forgotten Shapes project of recovering historically grounded Swiss type.
foundry_blurb: Forgotten Shapes present Texpo as a typeface designed by Gret Mengelt-Mergenthaler in 1964 for the Swiss National Exposition (Expo 64), held in Lausanne from April to October of that year. The typeface was revised in 2018 in collaboration with Christian Mengelt, and the foundry accompanies the release with extensive historical documentation of the exposition itself.

name: Normal-Grotesk
classification_notes: A revival of an early twentieth-century German grotesque originating at Wagner & Schmidt in 1908, circulated across Europe under many names; falls squarely within the historical grotesque tradition, sharing lineage with Akzidenz-Grotesk.
summary: A digital revival by Stephan Müller of a 1908 Wagner & Schmidt grotesque that circulated across Europe under at least a dozen different names, most famously as Aurora-Grotesk and Akzidenz-Grotesk.
description: The original design was developed at the Leipzig punchcutting firm Wagner & Schmidt around 1908 and subsequently sold as matrices to foundries across Germany, Austria, Italy and Sweden, each issuing it under its own name. Haas in Münchenstein cast it as Accidenz-Grotesk in the 1910s, then expanded and reworked it in the 1950s under the name Normal-Grotesk. Stephan Müller's revival, developed between 2011 and 2019, recovers that specific Haas iteration across five weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: Forgotten Shapes describe Normal-Grotesk as a sans-serif family rooted in a design developed at Wagner & Schmidt in Leipzig in 1908, whose matrices were sold to and reissued by foundries throughout Europe under names including Aurora-Grotesk, Cairoli, Klassische Grotesk and Akzidenz-Grotesk. The Haas foundry in Münchenstein expanded and reissued the design in the 1950s as Normal-Grotesk, the version that forms the basis of Stephan Müller's digital revival, developed from 2011 to 2019.

name: Neue moderne Grotesk
classification_notes: A revival of a nineteenth-century German grotesque originally cut at Wagner & Schmidt in 1908 and distributed across Europe under many names including Aurora-Grotesk and Akzidenz-Grotesk. Its regularised, upright forms with minimal contrast are characteristic of the pre-Helvetica European grotesque tradition.
summary: A digital revival of the 1908 Wagner & Schmidt grotesque, the same matrix-distributed design that circulated across Europe as Aurora-Grotesk, Akzidenz-Grotesk and Cairoli, among others.
description: Stephan Müller's revival, developed between 2011 and 2019, traces the grotesque back to its source: the Leipzig punchcutting firm Wagner & Schmidt, which sold matrices to foundries throughout Europe rather than casting type itself. The same design appeared under at least nine different names across Germany, Austria, Italy and Sweden before the Haas foundry reworked it into what became Akzidenz-Grotesk. Five weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, cover the family's pre-digital range in a form suited to contemporary use.
foundry_blurb: Forgotten Shapes document the tangled commercial history of this sans-serif, tracing it from Wagner & Schmidt's Leipzig punchcutting operation of 1908 through its distribution as Jupiter, Aurora-Grotesk, Cairoli, Akzidenz-Grotesk and many other names across foundries in Germany, Austria, Italy and Sweden. Stephan Müller worked on the revival from 2011 to 2019, drawing on surviving specimens from private collections to reconstruct a family that had long circulated under borrowed identities.
